repo.description: Vim-fork focused on extensibility and usability
repo.name: neovim

body:
<!--
  Thank you for contributing to Neovim!
  If this is your first time, check out https://github.com/neovim/neovim/blob/master/CONTRIBUTING.md#pull-requests-prs
  for our PR guidelines.
-->

## Problem
There is currently a significant gap compared to VSCode Remote SSH (#21635). Establishing a remote editing session via SSH requires manual bootstrap, tunnel configuration, and UI attachment (`:connect`), which disrupts the workflow.

## Solution
Implemented a native remote engine triggered by the `--remote-ssh` flag (or `:RemoteSSH` command), fulfilling the GSoC (2025) requirements for remote orchestration.

**Features:**
- `:RemoteSSH` command and Lua API (`select_and_connect`) using `vim.ui.select` to pick hosts parsed from `~/.ssh/config`, featuring full tab-completion and a graceful fallback to `vim.ui.input`.
- Queries the remote host (`uname`) to detect architecture (Linux/Darwin, x86_64/arm64) and verifies if the remote Nvim binary version (`api_level`, `prerelease` tag) matches the local host.
- If the remote host lacks an appropriate binary, injects a dynamic bash script to automatically download and symlink the correct tarball/appimage from GitHub Releases.
- Injects the local `stdpath('config')` to the remote host using a `tar` pipe, mapping it to a temporary `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME`.
- Leverages `ControlMaster` for all sequential SSH calls to bypass repeated cryptographic handshakes, significantly reducing startup latency. Windows targets/hosts fallback gracefully.
- Bypasses TTY hijacking during tunnel setup by passing `< /dev/tty` and intercepting password prompts via `stderr` back to `vim.ui.input` natively.
- `VimLeavePre` autocmd triggers remote socket and config cleanup through the multiplexed channel upon exit.

**Validation & Request For Testing:**
- Tested heavily on Linux -> Linux (Fedora and Debian). 
- Added URI parser and orchestration logic tests (`test/functional/lua/remote_spec.lua`).
- Added flag argument tests (`test/functional/core/remote_spec.lua`).
- Added diagnostics to `:checkhealth` for SSH and Multiplexing requirements.
- Included `remote_ssh.txt` in documentation.
- Verified picker UI flow and tab-completion for `:RemoteSSH`.
- **RFC:** *Windows targets/hosts include a graceful fallback mechanism (avoids `stty` and `ControlMaster`). macOS targets are supported via `.tar.gz` releases. I am seeking reviewers with Apple Silicon and Windows environments to help validate the bootstrap pipeline on those platforms.*

comment_data.body:
Just some early feedback: `:connect ssh://...` should work, and therefore a ` --remote-ssh` cli arg shouldn't be needed (nor wanted: generally we want to guide users to a single interface, and the `:` cmdline is the interface, while a few limited CLI args expose that interface from CLI).

Users that want to do stuff via cli can do:

    nvim -c 'connect ssh://root@server:2222'

and users that are already in an interactive session can do:

    :connect ssh://root@server:2222

Note that `:connect /path/to/socket` already works, but I think `:connect ssh://` isn't fully baked yet (which is something this PR could do).
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,139 @@
+*remote_ssh.txt*  Nvim
+
+                            NVIM REFERENCE MANUAL
+
+Remote SSH                                     *remote-ssh* *remote-ssh-plugin*
+
+Nvim allows orchestrating remote servers natively using SSH multiplexing. By
+passing the |--remote-ssh| flag or using the |:RemoteSSH| command, Nvim will
+automatically connect to a host, bootstrap itself, synchronize your local
+configuration, and connect the local UI to the remote headless server.
+
+                                      Type |gO| to see the table of contents.
+
+==============================================================================
+1. Introduction                                      *remote-ssh-introduction*
+
+The remote SSH engine uses `ssh` to communicate with the remote system. It:
+- Detects the remote architecture.
+- Bootstraps the Neovim binary in `~/.local/bin/nvim` and `~/.local/share/nvim-remote`.
+- Synchronizes your local `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/nvim` to the remote host.
+- Initiates a headless Neovim server and connects your local UI via a Unix
+  domain socket.
+
+==============================================================================
+2. Usage                                                    *remote-ssh-usage*
+
+From the command line: ~
+
+Connect to a remote server by passing the target host. Nvim will prompt for
+a password if needed, but SSH keys are highly recommended.
+>sh
+    nvim --remote-ssh user@192.168.1.100
+    nvim --remote-ssh ssh://root@server:2222

comment_data.body: `:connect` is intended for this (though it's not fully implemented, so this PR could do that), why do we need `:RemoteSSH` ?
comment_data.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,139 @@
+*remote_ssh.txt*  Nvim
+
+                            NVIM REFERENCE MANUAL
+
+Remote SSH                                     *remote-ssh* *remote-ssh-plugin*
+
+Nvim allows orchestrating remote servers natively using SSH multiplexing. By
+passing the |--remote-ssh| flag or using the |:RemoteSSH| command, Nvim will
+automatically connect to a host, bootstrap itself, synchronize your local
+configuration, and connect the local UI to the remote headless server.
+
+                                      Type |gO| to see the table of contents.
+
+==============================================================================
+1. Introduction                                      *remote-ssh-introduction*
+
+The remote SSH engine uses `ssh` to communicate with the remote system. It:
+- Detects the remote architecture.
+- Bootstraps the Neovim binary in `~/.local/bin/nvim` and `~/.local/share/nvim-remote`.
+- Synchronizes your local `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/nvim` to the remote host.
+- Initiates a headless Neovim server and connects your local UI via a Unix
+  domain socket.
+
+==============================================================================
+2. Usage                                                    *remote-ssh-usage*
+
+From the command line: ~
+
+Connect to a remote server by passing the target host. Nvim will prompt for
+a password if needed, but SSH keys are highly recommended.
+>sh
+    nvim --remote-ssh user@192.168.1.100
+    nvim --remote-ssh ssh://root@server:2222
+    nvim --remote-ssh myalias
+<
+
+From a running Neovim session: ~
+                                                                  *:RemoteSSH*
+
+`:RemoteSSH` [{uri}]
+
+Without arguments, `:RemoteSSH` presents a list of hosts discovered from
+`~/.ssh/config` using |vim.ui.select()|. Select a host from the list to
+connect.
+
+With an argument, `:RemoteSSH` connects directly to the specified URI:
+>vim
+    :RemoteSSH user@192.168.1.100

comment_data.body: this is not /core/ , should live in either `test/functional/plugin/` or more likely the existing `test/functional/lua/ssh_spec.lua` file

comment_regular_data.body:
Thanks for the incredibly swift feedback, Justin.

Adding more CLI flags is probably not the way to go - you're right on that. Integrating this directly into `:connect` makes a lot of technical sense to me.

I'll do my best to change the orchestration logic so that it connects to `:connect`. If it sees an `ssh:// prefix`, it will start the bootstrap and sync pipeline instead of just trying to connect to a raw socket.

As for `:RemoteSSH` vs `:connect:` I created the host picker (parsing `~/.ssh/config`) since I found it a pain to type URIs. Maybe I can make `:connect` present the host picker when called without arguments, or provide a completion-hub for it? @justinmk

> Maybe I can make `:connect` present the host picker when called without arguments

yep, exactly. actually, you might want to try https://github.com/neovim/neovim/pull/38454  , I haven't had a chance to look closely at it

> > Maybe I can make `:connect` present the host picker when called without arguments
> 
> yep, exactly. actually, you might want to try #38454 , I haven't had a chance to look closely at it

I've taken a dive into #38454, and to be honest, I find that approach a bit too heavy-handed on the C-side.

Extracting `cmdmods_dict()` and wiring the C execution loop directly to the peer discovery state feels like over-engineering for a UI workflow.

I was thinking about taking a stateless, lua-first approach, something like *pseudo-code* this:

1. `connect` (no args) drops straight into Lua (`vim.net._remote.select_host()`)
2. Lua globs `stdpath('run')` for live sockets (`sockconnect` quick check to filter out dead socket traps) and **merges** them with the ssh hosts parsed from the `~/.ssh/config`
3. it feeds that combined `[ssh]` and `[socket]` list to `vim.ui.select()`, so when the user picks a target, lua just loop back and fires `:connect <target>`.

So actually, from my side: no changes to `cmdmods.h` or C-level command modifiers, picker supports both local servers AND remote SSH orchestration in single UI and avoids the windows peer discovery limitations mentioned in that PR.

I found that slightly more elegant solution - testing it right now, something is coming up and looking promising - but let me know if you agree with this direction. Does that sounds right?

---
*P.S*. Sorry for the typos.
*P.P.S* I can drop some screenshots if you want to.

I think the first step is to add a `info:boolean` param to `serverlist()` in `runtime/lua/vim/_core/server.lua`, which returns detailed info. Then the `ui.select()` impl for `:connect` just calls that.

I'd suggest doing that as a separate pr, then rebase this one onto it.

@justinmk I have quick question regarding the remote lifecycle.

Currently, the teardown is strictly ephemeral: we sync the config to a temp directory and clean it up completely on exit. Trade-off is pretty brutal on latency for reconnects - I mean, plugin managers have to reclone everything and rebuild treesitter parsers from scratch every single time you connect.

I'm exploring to shift this to a persistent cache model (maybe anchoring the environment in `~/.local/share/nvim-remote`?):
We keep synced config, plugin state, and the downloaded `nvim` release. Teardown only kills the remote headless process and the SSH mux socket, leaving the data dir intact. Future connections just do a quick config hash/mtime check to skip the tar upload if nothing changed.

This would make reconnects near instant, similar to vscode remote handles its server footprint. Do you have any vision/preferences for keeping it strictly ephemeral with zero footpring, or is the cache approach somehow aligned with your current vision?

Just thinking out loud about the long-term direction while the current `serverlist` PR runs its course. No rush to implement this immediately.

title: feat(remote): implement native SSH orchestration (--remote-ssh)
